message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Strength and muscle mass are predictors of relevant clinical outcomes in critically ill patients, but in hospitalized patients with COVID-19 remains to be determined.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To investigate whether muscle strength or muscle mass are predictive of hospital length of stay (LOS) in patients with moderate to severe COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Prospective observational study.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Clinical Hospital of the School of Medicine of the University of Sao Paulo.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    One hundred ninety-six patients were evaluated. Ten patients did not test positive for SARS-CoV-2 during hospitalization and were excluded from the analyses. The sample comprised patients of both sexes (50% male) with a mean age (SD) of 59 (±15) years, body mass index of 29.5 (±6.9) kg/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    . The prevalence of current smoking patients was 24.7%, and more prevalent coexisting conditions were hypertension (67.7%), obesity (40.9%), and type 2 diabetes (36.0%). Mean (SD) LOS was 8.6 days (7.7); 17.0% of the patients required intensive care; 3.8% used invasive mechanical ventilation; and 6.6% died during the hospitalization period.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main outcome</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The outcome was LOS, defined as time from hospital admission to medical discharge.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The crude Hazard Ratio (HR) for LOS was greatest for handgrip strength comparing the strongest
                    <jats:italic>vs</jats:italic>
                    . other patients (1.54 [95%CI: 1.12 – 2.12; p = 0.008]). Evidence of an association between increased handgrip strength and shorter hospital stay was also identified when handgrip strength was standardized according to the sex-specific mean and standard deviation (1.23 [95%CI: 1.06 – 1.19; p = 0.008]). The magnitude of these associations remained consistent and statistically significant after adjusting for other covariates. Mean LOS was shorter for the strongest patients (7.5 ± 6.1 days)
                    <jats:italic>vs</jats:italic>
                    . others (9.2 ± 8.4 days). Evidence of associations were also present for vastus lateralis cross-sectional area. The crude HR identified shorter hospital stay for patients with greater sex-specific standardized values (1.17 [95%CI: 1.01 – 1.36; p = 0.037]); however, we found increased uncertainty in the estimate with the addition of other covariates (1.18 [95%CI: 0.97 – 1.43; p = 0.092]). Evidence was also obtained associating longer hospital stays for patients with the lowest values for vastus lateralis cross-sectional area (0.69 [95%CI: 0.50 – 0.95; p = 0.025). Mean LOS for the patients with the lowest muscle cross-sectional area was longer (10.8 ± 8.8 days)
                    <jats:italic>vs</jats:italic>
                    . others (7.7 ± 7.2 days).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions and Relevance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Muscle strength and mass assessed upon hospital admission are predictors of LOS in patients with moderate to severe COVID-19, which stresses the value of muscle health in prognosis of this disease.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Funding</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The authors acknowledge the support by the Brazilian National Council for Scientific and Technological Development (CNPq - grant 301571/2017-1). H.R. and B.G. are supported by grants from the Conselho Nacional de Pesquisa e Desenvolvimento (CNPq 428242/2018-9; 301571/2017-1; 301914/2017-6). B.G. is also supported by a grant from the Sao Paulo Research Foundation (FAPESP 2017/13552-2).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key points</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Question</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Do muscle strength and muscle mass predict hospital length of stay (LOS) in patients with moderate to severe COVID-19 patients?</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      In this prospective observational study that included 186 hospitalized patients with moderate to severe COVID-19, we observed that LOS was shorter among patients in the highest tertile of strength (assessed by handgrip)
                      <jats:italic>vs</jats:italic>
                      . those in the mid/lowest tertiles (crude Hazard Ratio [HR]: 1.54, 95%CI: 1.12-2.12). In addition, LOS was longer among patients in the lowest tertile of muscle cross-sectional area (assessed by ultrasound imaging)
                      <jats:italic>vs</jats:italic>
                      . those in the mid/highest tertiles (HR: 0.69, 95%CI: 0.50 – 0.95).
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Meaning</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Muscle strength and mass assessed on hospital admission are predictors of LOS in patients with moderate to severe COVID-19, suggesting that muscle health may be protective in this disease.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Muscle Strength and Muscle Mass as Predictors of Hospital Length of Stay in Patients with Moderate to Severe COVID-19: A Prospective Observational Study

reference.unstructured: Therneau T. A Package for Survival Analysis in R. R Package Version 3.1–12,. Accessed 11 June 2020,
Kassambara AK, M. ; Biecek P. Survminer : Drawing Survival Curves Using’Ggplot2’. R PackageVersion 0.4.6. Accessed11 June 2020,
